methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Chips & Semiconductors (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 14 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 20.6x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Teramount, a semiconductor-stage company founded by Hesham Taha and Avi Israel, raised $50M+ before acquisition. The chips and semiconductors sector comprises 14 same-sector peers total: 3 public comparables (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private peers including Cerebras, PsiQuantum, Etched, SambaNova Systems, and Credo Semiconductor.

Two standard valuation methods were tested. Peer-median analysis proved inapplicable: while 14 same-sector peers exist, zero fell into sufficiently narrow cohorts by stage, adjacency, or valuation tier—the minimum threshold for reliable triangulation is three datapoints per method. Public-comp anchoring also could not be applied; the three public semiconductor comparables trade at a median price-to-sales multiple of 20.6x, but Teramount's revenue figures are not on file, making revenue-based extrapolation impossible.

The absence of applicable quantitative methods reflects structural constraints in valuing pre-exit frontier semiconductor companies: tight pools of same-stage private peers, opacity around private revenue, and wide performance dispersion across the sector. Without disclosed financial metrics or a sufficiently dense comparable set, this analysis cannot support a specific valuation range. Any credible valuation would require either direct access to Teramount's revenue or EBITDA figures, or identification of additional same-stage private semiconductor peers with disclosed multiples. The acquisition outcome itself may provide more reliable guidance than these standard methods.
